You can get away with a little more internal stuff than you could before. In reality, we have more than two. We have two to everybody. And then we have the info letter to some, which gives us a third media that is arriving at a different time. What's your rule about what gets stuck in the envelope? It has to fit. I don't know. Do we have a rule? It's item number 32. Pardon me? I'm sorry? Is there always one major sales letter? No, not always. Is that because you don't get around to it? No, not because we don't get around to it. Because pretty much everything we do is by deliberate strategy. There are certain months that we don't want to do any over-offers and things like that. I think it was one month, I remember Dan wrote about a newsletter, says, see, I got through the whole newsletter and I didn't try to sell you one thing this time. Don't expect that again, but there is a lot of... There's an attitude of giving them a breather. There's an attitude of not overlapping like some other major promotion that's happening to that month and they're really getting slammed with something. And also you've got to understand that we have a tiered promotional agenda. So top tier are the two events, which each have fundamentally a six month marketing cycle. So super conference, summit, super conference, summit. So that takes precedence over virtually anything and everything. Because that's where the most money is and where the ascension is and all the other stuff. So I guess above all else what I would say to you, and by the way, if you took the same list and you went through the books, you'd find much of the same elements not only in each book, but you would find I do a pretty good job of hitting a lot of them in every chapter. I have a slightly different list, which I didn't bring you, but it's so close it doesn't make that much difference. And so what he just said is pretty important and I have it said to me by people in sort of a critical, acusatory way is that it appears that you do everything deliberately. Like there's something wrong with that, but they're right. I do everything deliberately. I found out a long time ago, so I got somebody coming for a day of consulting, and I like doing it at my house for a number of reasons including this. So I could think ahead of time, okay, John Duquesne's coming and we're going to talk about the catalog business and the health and fitness business and I could round up eight or ten things that he's probably going to want to see that I could show him and have him all in the neat little file folders in the conference room waiting for him when he was there. It's not near as impressive as if I go back into the back room and scrounge and find it for him spontaneously while he's there. It's much, he feels much better about what I went into the mouth of the bear and drug out for him and down on my knees, shuffling boxes and shit. So I could do it the other way. It's just not as effective. And so everything about this is pretty damn deliberate. Do I miss? Yeah, I miss, but not very often. And you get to be the judge in a minute. You used to be ten of these a year. Yeah, there was a time. I used to skip one, I think. Damn, I've been with you a long time. I only thought I skipped one. I lost and December or something like that. We ought to think about that. Longtown. It's twelve, right? Yeah, it's twelve. Yeah, it's twelve of everything. And it's 52 weekly faxes which somehow wind up 56, which I've never been able to figure the hell out of. It's been twelve for a long time. I mean, six, eight years. It hasn't been recently twelve. Yeah, now there's been significant change. I have 2002s here so you can draw a comparison. I mean, it's gotten more complex and sophisticated, I guess. No BS, no failure. Yeah. However, if you look, a lot of the same parts were there, you know, as are there now. But it has evolved, you know, over the years. And now, you know, audience is compelling changes on it as well. I'd like to make one comment. Doing things deliberate by strategy is, I'm always amazed. I mean, you know, what we do is pretty much, you know, right out there in the open, people can see. Because they see what we do. And I'm always amazed at how much they don't see that we do. That we do. Like, you know, we're doing a February, we're doing this seminar, one of the band strategies for seminars. And, you know, looking over the list of everything that we do, it's amazing. Everybody sees it. Well, they don't almost see it. But nobody sees it, you know. And there's like so much, that's the real difference in the success of the event is the strategy to like do everything that we do. Yeah. You know, I, my saying about that is, is all of our secrets are visible. But many are unseen. They're there, you know, they just don't, they don't attach any importance to, you know, it's like, you know, in the tapes, in the tapes with a platform selling boot camp I did with LeGrand, Ernie Kessler, who's deceased now, but Ernie was one of the guys there and Ernie did his platform presentation. And Ernie handles a glass of water in a particular way during his platform presentation. And Ernie handles the water exactly the same way, at exactly the same moment, on exactly the same word, every single time he does that presentation. Well, people see it, but they don't attach any importance to it. But it's not accidental. Ernie thought that all the way through and he's experimented with certain things and, you know, and to get that precisely correct. And so pretty much it's true. I mean, I try to be deliberate about everything I do. It seems to me it's not particularly smart to be accidental. But that, you know, the thing about the newsletters, see most people just sit down and write them. And we got to get one out and it's eight pages and what do we got laying around here? We can stick into it and, you know, on and on and on. And, you know, that's not the way to get the results. There's some more subtle stuff too, like Bill pointed out with the copy you did for me where I changed the fonts without even thinking that that was significant. See, I missed that. It's very significant. By the way, most copywriters don't do that for you. You know, I mean, it's not by the way they have to be matched exactly, but you don't want to deviate enormously from it. I came from topography, so a lot of copywriters don't have that background, but it's in my system. I only ask you a dumb question. What difference does it make with the fonts? Oh, it makes all the difference in the world. What? Fonts communicate. Well, what difference does it make how you dress on stage? Answer that question for me. Well, first impression, obviously. Yeah. Right? Well, go further. Well, the effectiveness of your communication is as far as their attention span. Whatever your message is, you've got to look like you're going to be... It makes a particular statement, right? And so there's lots of ways to make a statement. If you're like Halbert and you're really bold about it, so you're going to do massive takeaway selling. You've got people paid $7,000 to be in a room. You show up with beach shorts, sandals, a cap that says, Client, suck, and a t-shirt. And what are you trying to convey? You're trying to convey, I don't give a damn. And I don't want any clients, which was his consistent message from beginning to end. Thereby, signing clients as fast as he could sign clients, right? Dick Sutphen at the Past Life Regression Seminar shows up looking like a New Age guru at How to Build Your Metaphysical Business Seminar. He shows up looking like a business guy. Well, topography is the dress we put on the copy to be simplistic. And so, first of all, type, style, sizes, and formats, and cosmetics, bold-faced, okay? They communicate in and of themselves. So if I show you a typeface and don't let you read the words, there's still a certain level of communication that goes on there. Typewriter look stuff says, you sat down and did it, okay? Helvetica screams commercially prepared more than does anything else. Now, you might want to convey commercially prepared, right? A script conveys, in some cases, femininity, feminine authorship, or emotional type topic, right? So it's the dress for the type. Then it's about helping the reader through the piece. So one big long blur of all the same copy is tougher on people than this paragraph is bigger than this paragraph, and these two are indented, and this one isn't, you know, so forth. Now, again, I don't do it, I don't do much of it consciously anymore. I do it subconsciously. But I can consciously take one and make changes to it and help it, okay? So it matters a lot, you know, and the look of the newsletter matters a lot. Now, for the most part, I get what I want first time around because Carla's been doing it so long. So she, like, gets it, and I don't have to, I never do rough layouts. I just hear, you know. But I'll fix stuff afterwards and move this and change this and, you know, I want this in a fort, you know. So like a three or four column page.
